skill.name: ci-pipeline-setup
skill.description: Set up GitHub Actions CI/CD pipelines for linting, type checks, tests, builds, security audits, branch protection, and deployments.
skill.summary: Guides GitHub Actions CI/CD setup for reliable pull request checks and deployment automation.
skill.author: Doyajin174

security_audit.summary: Static command findings are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences and CI examples; they do not execute during skill use. The hardcoded URLs are official GitHub documentation links, and the GitHub token reference is a normal Actions secret placeholder. I found a moderate supply-chain concern: examples use tag-pinned actions, including a third-party Vercel action that receives secrets.

medium_findings.title: Tag-Pinned GitHub Actions Receive Deployment Secrets
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The deployment examples pass Vercel secrets to amondnet/vercel-action@v25. This is legitimate CI/CD guidance, but tag-pinned third-party actions can create supply-chain risk if the action is compromised or retagged.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The secret references and third-party action usage are explicit in the workflow examples. I did not find evidence of malicious exfiltration, so the concern is supply-chain exposure rather than confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Ruby Backticks
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The external command hits are Markdown headings and fenced YAML or JSON examples. They document CI workflows and package scripts, but they are not Ruby shell backtick execution inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible Markdown code fences, headings, or example block boundaries. No Ruby file or executable backtick expression is present in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: GitHub Token Reference Is a Workflow Placeholder
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The token reference uses GitHub Actions syntax for secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN in a labeler workflow example. It does not read local environment variables or collect credentials during skill execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token is shown inside a GitHub Actions YAML example and is scoped to the repository workflow. There is no surrounding code that sends the token to an unknown endpoint.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Official Documentation Links
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The network hits are references to GitHub documentation for Actions, branch protection, and Dependabot. They are not code paths that make outbound requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All cited URLs point to docs.github.com in a References section. No fetch, curl, webhook, or upload behavior appears at those locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Finding Is Unsupported
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The cited description line describes CI/CD pipeline setup and does not mention MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or another weak cryptographic primitive. No crypto use was found in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line contains plain skill metadata about GitHub Actions. I found no evidence of weak cryptographic algorithms in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Action Invoked With Repository Secrets
dangerous_patterns.description: The Vercel deployment examples invoke a third-party action by version tag while providing deployment secrets. Users should pin actions to commit SHAs and use least-privilege secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in the example workflow. The risk depends on user adoption and action trust, so confidence is high for the pattern but not for malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Manual checks slow releases and let regressions reach protected branches. This skill guides GitHub Actions workflows for linting, testing, building, security checks, and deployment.
actual_capabilities: Drafts GitHub Actions workflows for lint, type check, test, build, and audit jobs.
Suggests package scripts for Node.js projects using ESLint, Prettier, TypeScript, and Vitest.
Provides branch protection settings that require pull requests and passing status checks.
Shows matrix builds, dependency caching, PR labeling, and Dependabot examples.
Outlines production and preview deployment workflows for Vercel-based projects.
limitations: Focused mainly on GitHub Actions and Node.js project examples.
Does not verify that generated workflows match every repository layout.
Does not manage GitHub repository settings through the API by itself.
Deployment examples require users to create and scope their own secrets.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap CI for a New App
use_cases.description: Create a baseline workflow that checks formatting, types, tests, builds, and audit results before merge.

use_cases.title: Standardize Pull Request Gates
use_cases.description: Define required checks and branch protection guidance for teams that need consistent review rules.

use_cases.title: Add Preview and Production Deployments
use_cases.description: Plan deployment workflows that separate pull request previews from production releases on the main branch.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic CI
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a Node.js repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions CI workflow for my Node.js project with lint, test, type check, build, caching, and branch protection notes.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt Existing Scripts
prompt_templates.scenario: Match workflow jobs to package scripts already in the project.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my package scripts and design a CI workflow that uses the correct install, lint, test, type check, and build commands.

prompt_templates.title: Add Release Gates
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve merge quality with required checks and dependency automation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up branch protection, required status checks, Dependabot, and security audit guidance for a team using GitHub Actions.

prompt_templates.title: Design Deployment Automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced setup with preview and production deployment workflows.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design GitHub Actions workflows for CI, preview deployments on pull requests, production deployments on main, and safe secret handling.

output_examples.input: A new TypeScript web app needs reliable pull request checks.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a CI plan with separate lint, type check, test, build, and security audit jobs, plus required branch protection checks.

output_examples.input: A team wants faster CI without skipping tests.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests dependency caching, matrix testing choices, parallel jobs, and clear failure points for each verification stage.

output_examples.input: A project needs production and preview deployments.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines workflows for main branch production deploys and pull request preview deploys, including required repository secrets.

best_practices: Pin third-party GitHub Actions to trusted commit SHAs when secrets are provided.
Keep CI jobs small and named after the status checks required by branch protection.
Store deployment credentials only in GitHub Secrets with the smallest practical scope.
anti_patterns: Do not let maintainers bypass required status checks on protected branches.
Do not combine lint, tests, build, and deployment into one opaque job.
Do not expose deployment secrets to pull requests from untrusted forks.

faq.question: Does this skill support tools other than GitHub Actions?
faq.answer: The guidance is written for GitHub Actions. Concepts may transfer, but examples are not tailored for other CI systems.

faq.question: Can it create branch protection rules automatically?
faq.answer: It explains the settings and provides configuration guidance. Repository owners still need to apply rules in GitHub or through approved automation.

faq.question: What project types fit best?
faq.answer: The examples fit Node.js and TypeScript projects best, especially projects with lint, test, type check, and build scripts.

faq.question: Does it handle deployment secrets?
faq.answer: It shows where secrets are referenced in workflows. Users must create, protect, and rotate those secrets themselves.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation-focused, but it includes examples where third-party actions receive secrets by version tag.

faq.question: Can it help reduce CI runtime?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes caching, parallel jobs, and matrix build patterns that can reduce repeated work.
